bugs.error_message: Post-completion handler in commands/epic.md and .opencode/commands/epic.md instructed Claude to read .epic-worktrees/<repo>/<epic--name>/.epic-result.json, which never exists. The script writes to ${TMPDIR}/epic-toolkit/<repo>--<name>.result.json (different directory, different filename). Docs also claimed the file was retained only on failure, but the code retains it on both. Whenever an epic failed, Claude reported it could not find the result JSON.
bugs.root_cause: Two sources of truth for the result-file path: write_epic_result in run-sessions.sh and two command-doc files. The script's path was changed (moved out of the worktree so it survives cleanup) but the docs were not updated.
bugs.fix: Made the script the single source of truth: it now emits 'RESULT_FILE=<path>' as the first line of the [EPIC_RESULT_START]/[END] block on both success and failure. Both command docs were rewritten to read that line instead of hard-coding a path.

bugs.error_message: write_epic_result silently dropped sessions whose ids fell after a gap. With sessions [1, 2, 5] only sessions 1-2 appeared in the result JSON; session 5 vanished. Same when an epic failed in wave 1 of a three-wave plan — sessions in waves 2 and 3 had no SESSION_STATUS entry, so the loop hit a gap at sid=2 and broke immediately, reporting only the failed session.
bugs.root_cause: Both the data loop (line 1404) and the .errtype cleanup loop (line 1497) used 'for (( sid=1; sid<=999; sid++ )); [[ -z status ]] && break'. The break-on-empty pattern assumes contiguous, fully-populated session ids, which is not guaranteed: ids can be sparse by design and waves past a failure point never get a SESSION_STATUS entry at all.
bugs.fix: Replaced both loops with iteration over the DAG-derived id set: '_sids_sorted=$(printf %s\\n "${!SESSION_SLUG_BY_ID[@]}" | sort -n)' then a 'while IFS= read -r sid <<< "$_sids_sorted"' loop. Sessions with no SESSION_STATUS entry now default to 'pending' instead of vanishing, so unreached waves are visible in the result JSON. Verified with a standalone snippet using sparse ids [1,2,5,7] and missing statuses for 2 and 7 — old loop reported only sid=1, new loop reported all four with correct statuses.

bugs.error_message: epic-progress.py loops forever (spinning at ~5Hz on stderr) when the upstream stream-json producer ends with a partial line that has no trailing newline. Reproduced with a Python snippet that fed chunks ending in '...{"text":"hi"}' followed by EOF — the script never exits the main while.
bugs.root_cause: The break condition at the bottom of the main loop is `if eof and not line_buf.strip(): break`. The line-splitting loop only runs while `chunk is not None`, so on every post-EOF iteration `chunk_q.get` raises queue.Empty, chunk=None, the split block is skipped, and line_buf with non-empty trailing content is never drained. The break test stays false forever. Practically triggered when (a) a producer crashes / is killed mid-line (run-sessions.sh wave-timeout SIGKILL is one source — see bug-013), or (b) the final NDJSON line lacks a trailing newline. Combined with the orphaning behavior of `kill -9 $subshell_pid`, the python child can survive into the next epic run, burning CPU.
bugs.fix: Applied. On EOF (chunk is empty bytes), append a synthetic '\n' to line_buf before the parse-while-newline loop runs, so any trailing partial line is drained on the same iteration that sets eof=True. The existing `if eof and not line_buf.strip(): break` test then fires correctly. Verified by re-running the original Python repro: now exits at iteration 2 instead of looping forever.

bugs.error_message: run-sessions.sh truncates META values whenever the path or value contains a space. With a repo at /Users/Foo Bar/Code/myproj the META line emitted by epic-dag.py is `META operator_path=/Users/Foo Bar/Code/...`; after parsing OPERATOR_PATH becomes `/Users/Foo` and extract_prompt then aborts with `Could not extract operator prompt`. Reproduced with a 5-line bash snippet.
bugs.root_cause: The DAG-plan parser at line ~421 uses `set -- $line` (intentionally unquoted to word-split into positional params) to tokenize each line, then reads $2 / $3 / $4 etc. That word-split is space-delimited, so any field containing a space is silently broken across multiple positional params. Also affects SESSION rows (file, slug) and any other META key=value where the value may legitimately contain whitespace. epic-dag.py emits paths via `os.path.abspath(...).replace('\\', '/')` with no quoting/escaping, so it's fully on the bash side to handle.
bugs.fix: Applied. Replaced `set -- $line` with case/prefix-strip parsing in run-sessions.sh: META lines use `${line#META operator_path=}` etc. so values containing spaces are preserved verbatim. SESSION rows still word-split via `set -- ${line#SESSION }` because their fields (numeric ids, comma-separated deps, regex-derived slug, 0/1 flags, bare model/cli identifiers) are space-free by construction. Verified end-to-end with a fixture using `/Users/Foo Bar/Code/...` as operator_path — value now flows through to OPERATOR_PATH unmangled.

bugs.error_message: When a wave hits WAVE_TIMEOUT_MINUTES (default 240m), run-sessions.sh kills the in-flight session pids with `kill -9` and breaks out of the wait loop — but never reaps them, never sets SESSION_STATUS to failed, and never sets EPIC_FAILED. The merge phase finds status='running', falls through both `done` and `failed` cases silently, the outer wave for-loop continues to the next wave, and at the end the script logs `Epic completed successfully` and tries to open a PR. Reproduced by simulating the kill-and-break flow in a standalone bash script.
bugs.root_cause: The wave wait loop (line ~1732) handles timeout by iterating JOB_PIDS, sending SIGKILL, and `break`ing. There is no call to reap_finished_jobs after the kill, no manual SESSION_STATUS[$sid]='failed' update, and no `EPIC_FAILED=true`. Compounded by the fact that `kill -9 $pid` sends to a single PID; the run_one_session subshell's children (claude/opencode + the python progress pipeline) become orphans rather than being cleaned up, which interacts badly with bug-011 (the progress python can then spin forever).
bugs.fix: Applied. After SIGKILL, the timeout branch now iterates JOB_PIDS / JOB_SIDS, calls `wait` to reap each zombie, sets SESSION_STATUS=failed / SESSION_EXIT_BY_ID=137 / SESSION_ELAPSED_BY_ID, calls mark_session 'failed', updates FIRST_FAILED_ID, and finally sets EPIC_FAILED=true before breaking. Verified with a standalone bash fixture: two sleep-60 children → SIGKILL → both end up with status=failed/exit=137, EPIC_FAILED=true, FIRST_FAILED_ID=1. Did NOT switch to negative-pid (process-group) kill because the subshell was not started in its own pgid; orphan-child cleanup remains a separate issue (would need `setsid` or job-control mode).

bugs.error_message: Model not remapped when CLI is overridden without explicit model override. Session with `cli: opencode` and no model override would send invalid model flag to CLI (e.g., `--model sonnet` instead of `--model opencode/claude-sonnet-4`).
bugs.root_cause: In run_cli() function (lines 935-947), the default model was only remapped when CLI was overridden IF the model was also explicitly overridden. When a session overrode CLI but not model, the default model was never remapped for the new CLI, causing a model mismatch between the script's expectations and the CLI's supported model identifiers.
bugs.fix: Applied. Lines 939-946 now always remap the model when CLI is overridden, using the explicitly-overridden model if present, or the default MODEL if not. Changed from conditional remap (only if SESSION_MODEL_BY_ID set) to unconditional remap (always when SESSION_CLI_BY_ID set).

bugs.error_message: Worktree in-use detection fails silently on systems without lsof. If lsof is unavailable, is_worktree_in_use() always returns false, allowing concurrent epic runners to delete each other's worktrees and corrupt the run.
bugs.root_cause: Line 1676: `if command -v lsof >/dev/null 2>&1 && lsof +D "$sess_wt" 2>/dev/null | grep -q .` only checks if lsof exists; if it doesn't, wt_in_use stays false. No fallback detection method or error warning. Systems like macOS, BSD, or minimal Linux containers may not have lsof installed.
bugs.fix: Added new helper function is_worktree_in_use() that tries multiple detection methods in order: (1) /proc-based check for process CWD (Linux/Unix), (2) lsof (macOS/BSD), (3) fuser (alternative). Conservative fallback: if unable to determine, assumes not in use but logs the limitation. Updated line 1676 to call the helper instead of inline lsof check.

bugs.error_message: SESSION_FILE_BASENAME could be unset for sparse session IDs, causing corrupted MERGED_SESSIONS entries with missing filenames. PR body generation would then have malformed session list.
bugs.root_cause: Lines 1832, 1843, 1855 directly access SESSION_FILE_BASENAME[$sid] without validation. If SESSION_FILE_BASENAME is not populated (e.g., due to DAG parsing error or sparse session IDs), the array access returns empty string, creating MERGED_SESSIONS entries like "1 " with trailing space but no filename.
bugs.fix: Added helper function add_merged_session() that validates SESSION_FILE_BASENAME[$sid] is non-empty before appending to MERGED_SESSIONS. Warns and skips if missing. Replaced all three direct MERGED_SESSIONS+=() calls with add_merged_session() calls.

bugs.error_message: PR body generation produces malformed markdown when MERGED_SESSIONS is empty. Header reads '### Sessions' with nothing after it, creating confusing PR description.
bugs.root_cause: Lines 2059-2062 build SESSION_LIST by iterating MERGED_SESSIONS. If the array is empty (all sessions failed to merge), SESSION_LIST is empty string, and PR_BODY contains '### Sessions' followed by blank line. Not invalid markdown, but confusing to readers.
bugs.fix: Added check: if MERGED_SESSIONS is empty, set SESSION_LIST to '\n(No sessions were merged in this run)' instead of empty string. PR body now clearly indicates when no sessions merged.

bugs.error_message: is_worktree_in_use returns false-positive 'in use' when an unrelated process has a CWD inside a sibling directory whose path shares a prefix with the worktree (e.g., wt_dir=…/s01-foo, link_target=…/s01-foo-extra/src). The runner then refuses to clean up the legitimate stale worktree and exits 1, requiring manual intervention. Reproduced with a 5-line bash snippet.
bugs.root_cause: Line 44 used `[[ "$link_target" == "$wt_dir"* ]]` — a literal-prefix glob. Bash's pattern `"$wt_dir"*` matches `$wt_dir` followed by anything, so `…/s01-foo` matches both the real subdir `…/s01-foo/src` AND any sibling whose name starts with `…/s01-foo` such as `…/s01-foo-extra/src`. There was no separator anchoring on the boundary.
bugs.fix: Applied. Replaced with `[[ "$link_target" == "$wt_dir" || "$link_target" == "$wt_dir"/* ]]` — exact match OR strictly-under match. Verified with a fixture covering: prefix-shared sibling (no longer flagged), real subdir (still flagged), exact match (still flagged), unrelated path (not flagged), no-separator extension `…fooz` (no longer flagged).

bugs.error_message: extract_prompt silently truncates session prompts at the first nested ``` fence. Any session prompt containing an inner code example (```bash, ```python, etc.) had its content cut off at the inner closing ``` line. The model then received a partial prompt with no error, producing confusing failures attributable to 'the model didn't follow instructions' when it actually never saw them. Reproduced with a sample session file containing one inner code block.
bugs.root_cause: The awk script used `/^```$/ { if (capture) exit }` — any bare-triple-backtick line inside the captured region terminated capture. There was no fence-depth tracking, so an inner code block's closing ``` was indistinguishable from the outer prompt's closing fence. The template at docs/epic-prompt-template.md:93 documents the constraint, but the failure was silent (no warning, no error, just a truncated prompt).
bugs.fix: Applied. Rewrote the awk to track fence depth: lines matching `/^```[A-Za-z]/` (info-string openers like ```bash, ```python, ```diff) increment depth; bare `^```$` decrements depth if >0, else exits. Verified with three fixtures: language-tagged inner block, plain prompt (regression), multiple inner blocks. Bare-``` inner blocks remain ambiguous in CommonMark — template guidance to indent-instead still applies.

bugs.error_message: parse_frontmatter silently returns {} for any session file with CRLF line endings. The user's depends_on, parallel_safe, model, cli, and touches fields are all dropped. load_sessions then flags the file as no-frontmatter and falls back to the implicit linear chain, collapsing the entire parallel DAG into one-session-per-wave with no warning. Affects Windows/Git-Bash users and anyone whose checkout has core.autocrlf=true. Reproduced via direct call to parse_frontmatter with CRLF content.
bugs.root_cause:
Line 53 (`text.startswith("---
")`) and line 55 (`text.find("
---
", 4)`) hard-code LF separators. CRLF text starts with `---
`, so the startswith check fails and the function returns {} immediately. Cerebrum already calls Git Bash on Windows a target platform.
bugs.fix:
Applied. At the top of parse_frontmatter, normalise: `if '' in text: text = text.replace('
', '
').replace('', '
')`. One-line guard, runs only when CR is present. Verified parse output is identical for LF, CRLF, and mixed line endings.

bugs.error_message: Pre-flight stale-worktree cleanup deleted per-session worktrees from OTHER epics in the same repo. WORKTREE_BASE is keyed only by repo basename, so it is shared across every epic; the unscoped glob matched any sibling epic's worktree whose session id was not in the current DAG, including ones holding uncommitted work.
bugs.root_cause: Line 438 globbed `"$WORKTREE_BASE"/*--s[0-9][0-9]-*` without scoping by the current epic's branch. No is_worktree_in_use or unmerged-commit check on this code path either.
bugs.fix: Tightened the glob to `"$WORKTREE_BASE/${BRANCH_SANITIZED}--s"[0-9][0-9]-*` so only the current epic's worktrees are eligible. Verified with a two-epic fixture: bar's pre-flight no longer touches foo's worktrees.

bugs.error_message: extract_prompt returned empty for any session/operator file with CRLF line endings. Both the primary `$0 == "```md"` capture and the `/^---$/` frontmatter-stripping fallback failed because awk strips `\n` between records but keeps trailing `\r`. run-sessions.sh then exited with `Could not extract operator prompt` before any session ran on Windows / `core.autocrlf=true` checkouts.
bugs.root_cause: Same class as bug-024 (CRLF-vs-LF) but in the bash/awk path. Bug-024 fixed parse_frontmatter in epic-dag.py; the awk-based extract_prompt was overlooked because a successful DAG made it look like prompt extraction would also succeed.
bugs.fix: Added `sub(/\r$/, "")` as the first action in both awk programs so each record is normalised before any literal or anchored test. Verified four cases: CRLF md fence, CRLF frontmatter+title fallback, LF regression, and bug-023 nested-fence regression.

bugs.error_message: build_handoff_section emitted a `## Previous Session Handoffs` header plus the `Treat their file paths as the only memory of prior work` intro paragraph with zero entries when none of the parents had a handoff file on disk. The model received a confident header followed by nothing, inviting it to hallucinate the missing context.
bugs.root_cause: Header and intro were appended to `out` unconditionally before the parent loop ran; entries were appended only when find_handoff_for returned a path. If every lookup missed, `out` still contained the header.
bugs.fix: Refactored to accumulate parent matches into a separate `entries` variable and early-return when empty. The header is now built only when at least one parent contributed a real handoff. Verified: zero-handoff deps → empty output; one-of-two parents has handoff → header plus only that entry; deps='-' regression unchanged.

bugs.error_message: cleanup_merged_epic_branches refused to delete a stale epic branch whenever ANY sibling branch shared its name as a prefix and was checked out in a worktree. With epic/foo legitimately merged & closed, but epic/foo-bar checked out in another worktree, the in-use guard treated epic/foo as in-use and skipped it forever. Stale branches accumulate silently across runs.
bugs.root_cause: Line 201 used `grep -qF "branch refs/heads/$br"` against the `git worktree list --porcelain` output. `grep -F` matches the fixed string ANYWHERE on the line, so the search for `branch refs/heads/epic/foo` succeeds against the line `branch refs/heads/epic/foo-bar` (substring containment). The guard intended whole-line equality but didn't anchor.
bugs.fix: Applied. Changed to `grep -qFx`, which requires whole-line match. Verified with a porcelain fixture: epic/foo is no longer flagged when only epic/foo-bar is in the worktree; legitimate same-branch matches still detect correctly.

bugs.error_message: extract_prompt's frontmatter-fallback awk silently dropped the FIRST `# Heading` line found anywhere in the body when the body did not begin with a title. Files like `--- frontmatter --- / Description./ # Implementation Notes` lost the `# Implementation Notes` heading from the prompt. The model received the surrounding text but never the section header, leaving an apparent gap in instructions.
bugs.root_cause: The awk used a single `fm_done` flag set to 1 at the closing `---`, then `fm_done && /^# / { fm_done=0; next }` to swallow the title. Because intermediate non-`# ` content (description text, blank lines) didn't reset the flag, the swallow rule fired against the first `# ` heading encountered no matter how far into the body it appeared.
bugs.fix: Applied. Replaced the single fm_done flag with `awaiting_title` that is true only between the closing `---` and the first non-blank body line. Blank lines within the window are preserved; a `# ` heading in the window is stripped (the title); any other content closes the window so later `# Heading` lines pass through unchanged. Verified with two fixtures: (a) no-title body keeps `# Implementation Notes`; (b) with-title body strips the title but keeps later `# Section A`.

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py opened each session-NN-*.md file with bare `open(path)`, relying on the platform default text encoding. On Windows (cp1252) and any POSIX shell with `LC_ALL=C`/`PYTHONUTF8=0`, reading a session prompt that contains an em-dash, smart quote, or accented character (very common in AI-authored prompts) crashes the DAG with `UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii'/'cp1252' codec can't decode byte …`. The runner exits before any session has a chance to start, with a stack trace that doesn't obviously point at encoding.
bugs.root_cause: Line 135 used `with open(path) as f:` — no explicit `encoding=` arg. Python 3.x picks `locale.getpreferredencoding(False)` for that mode, which is platform/locale dependent. Other open() sites in the script (the inline init in run-sessions.sh, mark_session, write_epic_result) already pass `encoding='utf-8'`; this one was missed.
bugs.fix: Applied. Added `encoding="utf-8"` to the `open(path)` call. Verified: `PYTHONUTF8=0 LANG=C LC_ALL=C python3 scripts/epic-dag.py <dir> --bash` now succeeds on a session file containing `Café — résumé`, where before it raised UnicodeDecodeError mid-parse.

bugs.error_message: --retry cannot recover after the first failed attempt because run_one_session carries a nonzero rc into the next attempt. A later successful plan/exec path is skipped or still treated as failed.
bugs.root_cause: rc is initialized once before the retry loop. When an attempt sets rc=1, the next loop iteration never resets rc to 0 before planning. The execute pass is gated by `if [[ $rc -eq 0 ]]`, so a successful retry plan can still skip execution, and the break condition continues to see the stale failure.
bugs.fix: Applied. Reset rc to 0 at the start of each retry-loop iteration so a failed attempt cannot poison the next attempt. Verified with shell syntax checks.

bugs.error_message: --sequential is documented as one-session-per-wave / linear mode, but it only sets MAX_PARALLEL=1. Sessions from the same DAG wave still branch from the same trunk head and are merged only after the whole original wave completes.
bugs.root_cause: The flag changes throttling only. The DAG plan remains grouped into the original waves, and the wave loop merges children after all sessions in IN_RANGE are spawned/reaped. Serially throttled siblings therefore do not see prior siblings' commits.
bugs.fix: Applied. Rewrote the effective bash plan when --sequential is set so each session becomes its own synthetic wave, updates WAVE_IDS/SESSION_WAVE_OF/WAVE_COUNT, and makes show-dag/dry-run/banner render the effective sequential plan. Also bypassed strict sibling-overlap validation in sequential mode because there are no sibling waves after flattening. Verified with fixtures: normal dry-run shows 2 waves; --sequential dry-run shows 3 one-session waves; --sequential --strict passes overlapping touches while --strict without sequential fails.

bugs.error_message: Auto-commit fallback does not work on stock macOS because it unconditionally invokes GNU `timeout`, which is not available by default.
bugs.root_cause: run_cli guards optional session timeouts with `command -v timeout`, but the auto-commit fallback calls `timeout 60 git commit` directly. On Darwin/BSD shells without coreutils, that command returns 127 and the fallback commit is skipped.
bugs.fix: Applied. Added gtimeout support for session timeouts and changed the auto-commit fallback to use timeout/gtimeout when available, otherwise run git commit directly. Verified the empty timeout-wrapper expansion under bash -euo pipefail on stock macOS where neither timeout nor gtimeout is present.

bugs.error_message: `run-sessions.sh --dry-run` claimed no side effects but still provisioned OpenWolf merge-driver assets before the dry-run exit, creating `.gitattributes`, `.wolf/scripts/`, and `scripts/resolve-wolf.sh` in an OpenWolf repo.
bugs.root_cause: provision_wolf_merge ran immediately after REPO_ROOT resolution, before the show-dag/dry-run exits. Preview modes therefore mutated the repo before reaching the no-side-effects block.
bugs.fix: Applied. Wrapped provision_wolf_merge so it only runs when neither DRY_RUN nor SHOW_DAG is active. Removed the untracked files created by the verification run and re-ran the sequential dry-run fixture, confirming git status stayed unchanged.

bugs.error_message: Per-session `cli: claude` overrides silently passed an OpenCode-style model id to the Claude CLI when the global run was `--cli opencode --model sonnet`. Claude rejects `--model opencode/claude-sonnet-4` (unknown model), and the session fails with no obvious link to the cli swap. Reverse direction (claude→opencode) worked because the global MODEL was still in shorthand form.
bugs.root_cause: run-sessions.sh:247 mutated MODEL in place via `MODEL="$(map_model_shorthand "$MODEL" "$CLI_CMD")"`, so by the time run_cli's per-session remap ran, the user-supplied shorthand was already gone. The bug-016 fallback `${SESSION_MODEL_BY_ID[$sid]:-$MODEL}` then handed the already-mapped `opencode/claude-sonnet-4` to map_model_shorthand for cli=claude, which is a no-op for non-opencode targets. The mapping function is one-way once the model contains a `/`.
bugs.fix: Applied. Snapshot the un-mapped value into MODEL_RAW before the global remap, and use `${SESSION_MODEL_BY_ID[$sid]:-${MODEL_RAW:-$MODEL}}` in the per-session cli-override branch so the shorthand is re-mapped against the new cli. Verified with five fixtures covering both directions, model-only override, cli-only override, and the no-override baseline — every case now produces a cli-correct model id.

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py --strict false-positives on sibling directories whose names share a prefix. Two sessions declaring `touches: [src/foo/**]` and `touches: [src/foo-extra/**]` are flagged as overlapping and (with --strict) the run aborts with exit 2, even though no path is reachable from both globs. Same class as the bug-022 / bug-033 literal-prefix glob bugs in the bash side.
bugs.root_cause: globs_overlap()'s _stem comparison used `ap.startswith(bp) or bp.startswith(ap)` against directory stems with no separator anchoring. `src/foo-extra`.startswith(`src/foo`) returns True, so the stems are deemed overlapping despite living in disjoint dirs.
bugs.fix: Applied. Replaced the bare startswith with `_is_under(child, parent)` that requires either equality or `child.startswith(parent + '/')` — anchored on the path separator. Verified with five fixtures: prefix-shared siblings (now NO overlap), real subdir (still overlaps), exact match (still overlaps), unrelated dirs (no overlap), and `api/**` vs `api-v2/**` regression (no overlap).

bugs.error_message: Fast-finishing session jobs can make run-sessions.sh exit with rc=1 while leaving the session status stuck at running. Minimal repro: under `set -euo pipefail`, `pstate="$(ps -o state= -p "$pid" 2>/dev/null | tr -d ' ')"` exits the shell when `ps` returns no row for an already-finished child.
bugs.root_cause: reap_finished_jobs intends an empty ps result to mean the child vanished and should be waited, but the command substitution is a bare assignment under `set -e`. When `ps` returns nonzero, the shell exits before the subsequent `[[ -z "$pstate" || "$pstate" == Z* ]]` check can run.
bugs.fix: Applied. Guarded the ps pipeline with `|| true` so vanished children fall through to the existing wait/reap path. Verified with a minimal `set -euo pipefail` repro and a disposable fast-finishing --no-worktree epic run.

bugs.error_message: Running run-sessions.sh from repo A with a sessions directory in repo B can mutate repo A: it provisions OpenWolf files and can create/switch an epic branch before discovering the sessions path does not belong under the selected repo.
bugs.root_cause: REPO_ROOT is resolved from the current working directory, not from the sessions directory. The only under-repo guard is inside trunk worktree setup after provisioning side effects, and that guard prints `err` without exiting.
bugs.fix: Applied. Resolve and validate `ORIG_SESSIONS_DIR` under `ORIG_REPO_ROOT` before OpenWolf provisioning, branch creation, or worktree setup; exit nonzero on mismatch. Also added a defensive exit to the later canonicalization guard. Verified an external sessions path exits rc=1 and leaves the branch unchanged.

bugs.error_message: If one successful sibling hits a non-auto-resolvable merge conflict, the wave merge loop sets EPIC_FAILED but continues iterating and may merge later sibling branches into trunk before halting.
bugs.root_cause: The merge-conflict branch sets `EPIC_FAILED=true` and `FIRST_FAILED_ID`, then falls out of the case block without breaking the `for sid in IN_RANGE` loop. The halt check runs only after every sibling has been considered.
bugs.fix: Applied. Added an immediate `break` after a non-auto-resolved merge conflict sets EPIC_FAILED, preventing later siblings in the same wave from being merged after the first conflict. Verified shell syntax.

bugs.error_message: --no-worktree successful runs can reach cleanup with TRUNK_SESSIONS_REL unset, causing `set -u` to abort at the session scaffolding cleanup step.
bugs.root_cause: TRUNK_SESSIONS_REL was assigned only inside the worktree setup branch. The --no-worktree branch set TRUNK_SESSIONS_DIR but never populated the relative path later used by cleanup for `_EPIC_SLUG`, git rm, and rm -rf.
bugs.fix: Applied. Compute ORIG_SESSIONS_REL once after validating the sessions directory and assign TRUNK_SESSIONS_REL in both worktree and --no-worktree paths. Verified a disposable --no-worktree run completes rc=0 and performs cleanup.

bugs.error_message: merge-wolf-json.py crashes with UnicodeDecodeError mid-merge on Windows (cp1252) and any POSIX shell with LC_ALL=C / PYTHONUTF8=0 whenever .wolf/buglog.json contains an em-dash, smart quote, or accented character — extremely common because buglog entries paraphrase AI-authored prose. Git records the file as unresolvable and the user sees an opaque merge failure with no obvious link to encoding. Same class as bug-035, which the cerebrum specifically flagged with 'audit every open( site, not just the ones in the file you're editing — sibling helpers often have the same bug'. This sibling helper was missed.
bugs.root_cause: load() called Path(path).read_text() and write() called Path(path).write_text(...) with no `encoding=` argument. pathlib falls back to locale.getpreferredencoding(False), which is cp1252 on Windows and ASCII when LC_ALL=C / PYTHONUTF8=0. Any non-ASCII byte in the file (or in data being written) raises UnicodeDecodeError / UnicodeEncodeError before merge_buglog can run.
bugs.fix: Applied. Pinned encoding='utf-8' on both Path.read_text and Path.write_text in scripts/wolf-merge/merge-wolf-json.py. Added UnicodeDecodeError to the load() except clause so a non-utf-8 file is treated as 'unreadable' (returning None) rather than crashing the whole driver. Verified end-to-end: merge driver invoked under PYTHONUTF8=0 LC_ALL=C LANG=C with ours.fix='Café — résumé' and theirs.fix='naïve façade ☃' now returns 0 and produces a merged buglog with both entries' utf-8 bytes preserved.

bugs.error_message: Every CLI error surfaced through the [EPIC_RESULT_START]/[END] block and the result JSON's sessions[].error_detail field shows visible literal backslashes — e.g. an error like 'Bad input: "foo"' renders as 'Bad input: \\"foo\\"' when the user reads the JSON, and a path with a single backslash gets doubled. Cosmetic but confusing; it leaks into anything that re-emits error_detail (CI dashboards, the orchestrator's failure summary, on-call playbooks).
bugs.root_cause: classify_error pre-escaped the captured grep -oE error message with `sed 's/\\/\\\\/g; s/"/\\"/g; s/\t/\\t/g'` before writing to .errtype. write_epic_result then opens that file in Python and stores the contents in entry['error_detail']; json.dump escapes them AGAIN. Net effect: every literal backslash and double quote in the original error is doubled in the JSON-encoded output, and round-tripping the JSON yields '\"' instead of '"'. The bash escaping was redundant from the start because Python is the only consumer and json.dump already escapes for JSON.
bugs.fix: Applied. Removed the sed pre-escape in classify_error and switched the redirection to printf '%s\n' so .errtype contains the raw single-line error verbatim. write_epic_result's existing json.dump now performs the only escaping, producing well-formed JSON that round-trips cleanly. Verified with a fixture log containing 'Bad input: "foo" said the parser': .errtype now holds the unescaped string and json.dumps encodes it as 'Bad input: \"foo\" said the parser' (one backslash, the JSON escape).

bugs.error_message: On Windows where `python3` is the Microsoft Store stub (passes `command -v` but errors on actual invocation) and on systems where only `python` is installed, every wolf-json merge silently fails. Git invokes the registered driver, the interpreter doesn't run, the file is left unresolved, and the user sees a 'merge conflict' on .wolf/buglog.json / hooks/_session.json with no obvious link to the missing interpreter. run-sessions.sh already detects this exact scenario for its own python invocations (probing python3 then python with --version), but install-merge-driver.sh hard-codes `python3` in the registered git config string.
bugs.root_cause: The script wrote `git config merge.wolf-json.driver "python3 \"$DRIVER\" %O %A %B %P"` unconditionally. It never probed whether `python3` could actually run, and the git config value persists across invocations — so even if the user later installs a real python3 on a different PATH, the registered driver still points to whatever `python3` resolves to at merge time (and on Windows that's typically the Store stub that errors with no helpful message).
bugs.fix: Applied. Added the same probe used in run-sessions.sh: try `python3 --version` first, fall back to `python --version`, exit 0 (no registration) if neither runs. Stored the chosen interpreter in PY_CMD and substituted it into the driver config string. Verified end-to-end with PATH limited to a tempdir that exposes only `python` (no `python3`): registered driver is now `python "..." %O %A %B %P` instead of the broken `python3` form. Re-running on macOS with python3 present preserves the python3 selection.

bugs.error_message: Three open() sites in scripts/lib/epic-result.sh and scripts/run-sessions.sh still relied on locale.getpreferredencoding(False), so the same UnicodeDecodeError class that took down epic-dag.py (bug-035) and merge-wolf-json.py (bug-049) lived on in the result-reporting and status-init paths. Reproduced under PYTHONUTF8=0 LC_ALL=C LANG=C against a tmp_merged file containing 'session-01-café-résumé.md' / 'session-02-naïve-façade-☃.md': the bare open() at epic-result.sh:150 crashed mid write_epic_result with `UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xc3`, the [EPIC_RESULT_START]/[END] block was never emitted, and external watchers polling RESULT_FILE never saw a sentinel. Sibling-helper bug — exactly the audit that the cerebrum 2026-05-05 entry for bug-035 said to do across every open( site, not just the file being edited.
bugs.root_cause: Three places opened text files without encoding='utf-8', falling back to platform default (cp1252 on Windows, ASCII under LC_ALL=C / PYTHONUTF8=0): (a) epic-result.sh:150 read the bash-written tmp_merged file (lines like '1 session-01-foo.md' — filenames are user-supplied and frequently carry em-dashes / accents in AI-named sessions), this is the active-crash site; (b) epic-result.sh:204 read back the just-written result.json for terminal pretty-printing — latent because json.dump defaults to ensure_ascii=True so the file is pure ASCII today, but a future ensure_ascii=False would surface it; (c) run-sessions.sh:682 wrote the initial .epic-status.json — also latent for the same ensure_ascii reason. The audit cerebrum entry warned 'sibling helpers often have the same bug' after bug-035 (and again after bug-049 in merge-wolf-json.py); these three sites in the result/status path were missed both times.
bugs.fix: Applied. Pinned encoding='utf-8' on all three sites: `open(sys.argv[3], encoding='utf-8')` for the merged-sessions read (epic-result.sh:150), `json.load(open(sys.argv[1], encoding='utf-8'))` for the result-print read (epic-result.sh:204), and `open(sf, 'w', encoding='utf-8')` for the status-init write (run-sessions.sh:682). Verified the active-crash site by writing a tmp_merged fixture from bash with 'café-résumé' / 'naïve-façade-☃' filenames and reading it under PYTHONUTF8=0 LC_ALL=C LANG=C: pre-fix raised UnicodeDecodeError on byte 0xc3 mid-read, post-fix returned `read OK, count = 2 byte-len of first = 27`. Verified bash -n on all three modified shell scripts.

bugs.error_message: cleanup_merged_epic_branches never deletes a stale `epic/*` branch that has no PR but is fully merged on the remote, whenever the user's local default branch lags origin. After `git fetch --prune origin` updates origin/main but does NOT fast-forward local main (the common case in a worktree-based workflow where main is rarely checked out), the no-PR fallback runs `git merge-base --is-ancestor "$br" "$default_branch"` against the LOCAL ref, gets a false negative, and silently keeps the branch forever. Stale `epic/*` branches accumulate across runs with no surfaced error. Reproduced with a scratch bare-remote / clone fixture: origin/main fast-forwarded to epic/foo's tip, local main left at the original commit, fetch+prune run, then the guard checked — pre-fix `WOULD KEEP (stale!)`, post-fix `WOULD DELETE`.
bugs.root_cause: Line 218 used `merge-base --is-ancestor "$br" "$default_branch"` where `$default_branch` is the bare branch name returned by `gh repo view --json defaultBranchRef -q '.defaultBranchRef.name'` (e.g. 'main'). Bare names resolve to the LOCAL ref refs/heads/main, but the only ref this code path can rely on being current is `origin/<default_branch>` — that's what the `git fetch --prune origin` immediately above just updated. The mismatch was invisible whenever the user's local main happened to be up-to-date, which is rare in a worktree workflow.
bugs.fix: Applied. Resolve a `default_ref` that prefers `origin/$default_branch` when that ref exists (verified via `git rev-parse --verify --quiet`), falling back to the bare local name only if no remote-tracking ref is present (e.g. detached/no-origin repos). The is-ancestor guard now compares against the same ref `git fetch --prune` just updated. Verified with a scratch bare-remote/local-clone fixture (origin/main fast-forwarded but local main left behind): pre-fix the guard answered 'keep stale!', post-fix 'delete'. bash -n clean.

bugs.error_message: _realpath silently returns unresolved input on systems where realpath, grealpath, AND `python3` are all unavailable
bugs.root_cause: Line 20 of `_realpath` uses a hardcoded `python3 -c "..."` as the third fallback, with no `python` probe. Same anti-pattern that bug-051 fixed in scripts/wolf-merge/install-merge-driver.sh and that run-sessions.sh:259-267 guards against (probes both `python3 --version` and `python --version`). On Windows/Git-Bash where `python3` is the Microsoft Store stub that satisfies `command -v` but errors on real invocation, or on minimal Linux containers where only `python` (not `python3`) exists AND neither `realpath` nor `grealpath` is on PATH, the chain falls through silently to `|| echo "$1"` and returns the raw input. ORIG_REPO_ROOT and ORIG_SESSIONS_DIR then carry un-canonicalized paths, breaking the `${SESSIONS_DIR#$REPO_ROOT/}` prefix strip downstream and tripping the case-mismatch guard at run-sessions.sh:580 with a misleading 'install GNU coreutils' diagnostic.
bugs.fix: Probe both python interpreters before falling back to `echo "$1"`. Either duplicate the run-sessions.sh probe inline (`command -v python3 && python3 --version` then `command -v python && python --version`), or expose `PYTHON_CMD` as an env var that the lib reads. The probe-inline approach is self-contained and matches the pattern install-merge-driver.sh now uses post bug-051.

bugs.error_message: .epic-config.json silently overrides user CLI flags when the user explicitly passes the default value
bugs.root_cause: Lines 162-188 use each variable's default value as a sentinel for 'the user did not pass this CLI flag': `[[ "$TIMEOUT" -eq 0 ]]`, `[[ "$RETRY" -eq 0 ]]`, `[[ "$MAX_PARALLEL" -eq 4 ]]`, `[[ "$MODEL" == "sonnet" ]]`, `[[ "$AUTO_COMMIT" == "true" ]]`, `[[ "$AUTO_PR" == "true" ]]`, `[[ "$SKIP_PLAN" == "false" ]]`, `[[ "$KEEP_WORKTREE" == "false" ]]`. When a user explicitly passes the same value as the script default — most notably `--timeout 0` (intent: disable timeout), `--max-parallel 4`, or `--model sonnet` — the parse-args loop assigns that value, and these checks can no longer distinguish 'user typed it' from 'user omitted the flag'. The config file's value silently wins, contradicting the user's explicit intent. Worst case: a repo with `"timeout": 30` in config will enforce a 30-minute per-session timeout even when the user passed `--timeout 0` to disable it. The CLI_OVERRIDE check at line 168 escapes this trap because its default is the empty string, which the user can't realistically pass.
bugs.fix: Either (a) introduce per-flag *_USER_SET booleans set to true inside the parse-args case branch and gate the config override on `! $*_USER_SET`, or (b) load the config block BEFORE the CLI parse loop so CLI flags override config rather than the other way around. Option (b) is cleaner — the parse loop already overwrites whatever defaults are in scope, so config-loaded values just become the new defaults.

bugs.error_message: epic-poll-progress.py is documented in CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, README.md, and anatomy.md as the OpenCode fallback but is never invoked
bugs.root_cause: scripts/run-sessions.sh:333 unconditionally sets `PROGRESS_SCRIPT="$(dirname "$0")/epic-progress.py"` and never references epic-poll-progress.py. scripts/lib/epic-session.sh:204-247 only ever pipes into `$PROGRESS_SCRIPT`, so the polling fallback is dead code. The OpenCode branch in run_cli (lines 232-251) uses the same stream-json-style epic-progress.py — fine in practice because OpenCode's `--format json` output is also newline-delimited JSON that epic-progress.py recognises via its `step_start` / `tool_use` / `text` / `step_finish` event handlers — but the README and CLAUDE.md still describe the polling script as the OpenCode fallback, which misleads anyone reading the architecture overview and confuses anyone trying to extend the progress display for a CLI that *genuinely* lacks streaming output.
bugs.fix: Pick one: (a) delete scripts/epic-poll-progress.py and remove its mentions from CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, README.md, and .wolf/anatomy.md if the polling fallback is genuinely obsolete; or (b) wire it back in for a real stream-json-incapable CLI by selecting between PROGRESS_SCRIPT variants in run_cli based on `session_cli`. Option (a) matches current behaviour with no regression risk.

bugs.error_message: mark_session status lock leaked by SIGKILL; all subsequent calls silently no-op, live UI goes stale
bugs.root_cause: mark_session acquires an exclusive lock file (.lock) and releases it in a Python finally block. SIGKILL (used by wave-timeout at line 861) bypasses finally, leaving the stale lock file on disk. All subsequent mark_session calls spin 200x50ms then sys.exit(0) without updating the status JSON, so the live UI permanently shows the killed sessions as 'running'.
bugs.fix: Added stale-lock detection in the retry loop: if the lock file is older than 60 seconds, unlink it and let the next iteration claim it. The 60s threshold is far longer than any healthy lock hold time.

bugs.error_message: Session filename with space in slug breaks bash SESSION line field parsing; wrong IDs, branch names, and model assignments
bugs.root_cause: SESSION_RE matches slug as (.+) which allows spaces. emit_bash writes space-separated fields: 'SESSION <wave> <id> <file> <deps> <slug> <parallel> ...'. A slug with a space (e.g. from session-01-my api.md) causes set -- $line in run-sessions.sh to misalign every positional parameter after the slug.
bugs.fix: Added explicit space check after slug extraction in load_sessions(); raises SystemExit with a clear rename hint before the DAG is built, so the error surfaces immediately rather than causing a corrupted run.

bugs.error_message: Wave timeout (line 852) kills only the bash subshell PID; the claude CLI and python progress.py children are reparented to init and continue running — they can write into worktrees the orchestrator is about to delete, burn API tokens for sessions that were supposed to be cancelled, and survive across runs
bugs.root_cause: The kill loop at lines 858-870 does `kill -9 "$_tpid"` where $_tpid is the PID of the `( run_one_session ... ) &` subshell. The subshell itself dies, but `kill -9 <pid>` (positive PID) does NOT propagate to children. Inside run_one_session → run_cli the actual workhorse processes are a pipeline (`claude -p ... | python3 epic-progress.py ...`), all of which are children of the subshell, not the orchestrator. With job control off (default in non-interactive bash) and no setsid wrapper, those children share the orchestrator's process group, so `kill -- -$pgid` would kill the orchestrator itself. After `kill -9 "$_tpid"` they are simply reparented to PID 1 and keep running.
bugs.fix: Before (or instead of) killing the subshell, walk and kill its descendants. Cheapest portable form: `pkill -9 -P "$_tpid" 2>/dev/null || true` immediately followed by `kill -9 "$_tpid"`. For bash 3.2/macOS without pkill, fall back to `pgrep -P "$_tpid" | xargs -r kill -9` or a recursive /proc walk. The cleaner fix is to launch each session subshell under its own process group via `setsid` and then `kill -9 -- -$pgid` on timeout, which also covers grandchildren.

bugs.error_message: epic-progress.py update_status_file leaks .epic-status.json.lock when the progress process is killed by SIGKILL (wave timeout, OOM); subsequent updates silently no-op for the rest of the run because the lock file is never reclaimed
bugs.root_cause: update_status_file (lines 29-60) acquires the lock with O_CREAT|O_EXCL|O_WRONLY, releases it in `finally`, and retries 100×0.02s if it cannot acquire. The bug-061 fix added a STALE_SECS=60 age check to `mark_session` in epic-session.sh so a SIGKILL-leaked lock auto-recovers on the next call. update_status_file is the *exact same lock pattern on the exact same lock file* (`<status_file>.lock`) but never received that fix. SIGKILL bypasses `finally`; the lock survives indefinitely. After ~2s of retry the function returns silently, so the live UI dashboard freezes — every progress event from every later session disappears with no log line, no warning, and no exit code.
bugs.fix: Mirror the mark_session pattern: in the except branch, do `if time.time() - os.path.getmtime(lock) > STALE_SECS: os.unlink(lock)` (with try/except OSError) before the `time.sleep(0.02)`. Keep STALE_SECS aligned with mark_session (currently 60). This is the audit-every-site discipline cerebrum already calls out for bug-035; the same discipline applies to lock files, not just open() calls.

bugs.error_message: UI dashboard process is leaked when the runner aborts at the per-session worktree pre-flight checks (worktree-in-use or unmerged-commits); the python TUI keeps redrawing on the user's terminal after the runner has exited, requiring a manual kill
bugs.root_cause: The live UI is launched at line 698-706 (UI_PID=$!), and explicitly killed only in the post-wave shutdown block at lines 974-978. Between those two points the script has two hard exits — `exit 1` at line 800 (worktree in use by another process) and `exit 1` at line 811 (per-session branch has unmerged commits ahead of trunk). Both exits run AFTER the UI was started but BEFORE the kill block, and the EXIT trap installed at line 347 only does `rm -f "$DAG_TMP"` — it does not touch UI_PID or any backgrounded job. Result: the orchestrator dies, the python TUI keeps running, and the terminal stays clobbered until the user finds the PID. Any future hard exits added between line 706 and 974 will inherit the same defect.
bugs.fix: Expand the EXIT trap to also clean up the UI and any spawned jobs, e.g.: `trap '_rc=$?; [[ -n "${UI_PID:-}" ]] && kill "$UI_PID" 2>/dev/null; [[ ${#JOB_PIDS[@]:-0} -gt 0 ]] && { pkill -P $$ 2>/dev/null; }; rm -f "$DAG_TMP"; exit $_rc' EXIT`. This makes the cleanup defensive against any future exit path rather than relying on every author to remember to kill the UI manually before each new `exit 1`.

bugs.error_message: After wave timeout or runner abort with --timeout MINS set, claude/opencode child process is reparented to init and keeps running (orphan), continuing to burn API tokens and hold worktree handles.
bugs.root_cause: When TIMEOUT > 0, run_cli builds the pipeline as `timeout DURATION env -u CLAUDECODE claude ... | python progress.py`. The session subshell forks `timeout` as a direct child; `timeout` in turn forks `claude` (env execs into claude inside that child). So claude is a GRANDCHILD of the subshell, not a direct child. `pkill -9 -P $subshell_pid` (used in both _on_exit at run-sessions.sh:357-368 and the wave-timeout cleanup at run-sessions.sh:879-896) only matches direct children. After SIGKILLing `timeout`, claude's parent dies and claude is reparented to PID 1 — it survives with no one waiting on it. Reproduced locally with `sh -c 'sleep 30 & wait' &; pkill -9 -P parent; kill -9 parent` — sleep persists with PPID=1. The bug-063 cerebrum entry described the symptom (orphaned children) but the fix only walks one process-tree level.
bugs.fix: Walk the process tree before killing. Replace the single `pkill -9 -P` with a recursive helper, e.g.: `kill_tree(){ local p=$1; for c in $(pgrep -P $p 2>/dev/null); do kill_tree $c; done; kill -9 $p 2>/dev/null||true; }`. Apply at both call sites (EXIT trap and wave-timeout). Or: spawn each session subshell via `setsid` so the whole subshell tree is its own process group, and kill with `kill -9 -<pgid>` (negative pid). The setsid approach is cleaner but requires checking macOS behaviour.

bugs.error_message: Pre-PR rebase reports REBASE_RESULT=ok and pushes the un-rebased branch when `git rebase` fails fast (dirty tree, missing target ref, etc.) without leaving rebase-merge/rebase-apply state.
bugs.root_cause: rebase_with_wolf_resolve() at epic-git.sh:146-191 has three return paths to 0: (1) already a descendant; (2) clean rebase succeeded; (3) the function falls off the end after the conflict-resolve while-loop. Path 3 is reached whenever the initial `git rebase ...; then return 0; fi` fails AND no rebase state directory exists for the while-loop to enter. That happens when git rebase rejects the operation early — verified locally: `git rebase nonexistent-branch` exits 128, prints `fatal: invalid upstream`, and leaves NO `.git/rebase-merge` or `.git/rebase-apply`. In that case the while loop body never runs and the function returns 0. The caller at run-sessions.sh:1115-1117 then sets REBASE_RESULT=ok, prints `Rebased onto origin/<default> — PR will be fast-forward-mergeable`, and force-pushes the un-rebased branch with --force-with-lease (line 1148). The PR shows the un-rebased history while the user is told it was rebased.
bugs.fix: Track whether a rebase was actually performed. After the initial `git rebase` attempt fails, check for rebase state BEFORE entering the while loop and treat 'no state' as a hard failure: `if ! git -C "$workdir" rev-parse --git-path rebase-merge | xargs test -d && ! git -C "$workdir" rev-parse --git-path rebase-apply | xargs test -d; then return 1; fi`. Or capture the initial rebase exit code and propagate it when no state dir exists. Either makes REBASE_RESULT=conflict and falls through to a non-force push, which is the safe behaviour.

bugs.error_message: Plan-phase cache (`[[ "$plan_file" -nt "$session_path" ]]`) is silently invalidated on every re-run. The pre-wave `cp "$ORIG_SESSIONS_DIR"/session-*.md "$TRUNK_SESSIONS_DIR/"` at run-sessions.sh:632 omits `-p`, so each run resets the destination's mtime to "now". Any cached plan from a prior run has an EARLIER mtime than the freshly-copied session prompt, so plan_cached=false and the entire plan phase re-executes. Empirically reproduced: dest.md mtime is 11:10:20, .plan.md mtime is 11:10:18 -> .plan.md is NOT newer than dest.md. The cache the comment promises ("Reuse a cached plan file if it exists, is non-empty, and is newer than the session prompt") never fires across runs - exactly the case it was designed for (resume after a session executed plan but failed exec).
bugs.root_cause: `cp` defaults to setting destination mtime = now, not preserving source mtime. The plan-cache freshness check at scripts/lib/epic-session.sh:375 compares plan_file mtime against session_path mtime, so the freshly-copied session always looks newer than any pre-existing plan, even when neither the source session nor the plan changed.
bugs.fix: Use `cp -p` (or rsync with -t / install -m644 -p) on run-sessions.sh:632 so the destination's mtime tracks the source's. Then the cache invalidates only when the user actually edits the session prompt (the documented intent), not on every cp.

bugs.error_message: When a session declares `cli:` in frontmatter without also declaring `model:`, the session's CLI override is silently lost AND the literal CLI name (e.g. "claude") is parsed as the per-session model id. epic-dag.py emit_bash prints `SESSION <wave> <id> <file> <deps> <slug> <parallel> <model> <cli>` with empty model rendered as a blank field, producing two adjacent spaces. run-sessions.sh:410-412 splits via `set -- $_rest` (unquoted), so consecutive whitespace collapses and the cli value shifts into $7, which is assigned to smodel. Result: SESSION_MODEL_BY_ID[$sid]="claude" (wrong), SESSION_CLI_BY_ID[$sid]="" (wrong). In run_cli (epic-session.sh:162), the model-override branch fires with raw_model="claude" and map_model_shorthand turns it into either bare "claude" or warns "not a known OpenCode shorthand"; the cli-override branch never fires (-n test on empty fails) so the global CLI is still used. The user's per-session `cli: claude` selector is silently dropped.
bugs.root_cause: Bash field-splitting on whitespace collapses consecutive separators. The bash protocol between epic-dag.py and run-sessions.sh has trailing optional fields (model, cli) but uses bare single-space delimiters, so an empty middle field shifts every later field one slot to the left. Same family as bug-012 / bug-062 (positional space-delimited protocol bugs).
bugs.fix: Two viable repairs: (a) in emit_bash, render empty optional fields as a sentinel (e.g. `-`) and have run-sessions.sh translate `-` back to empty after `set --`; or (b) switch the SESSION line to a delimiter that survives empty fields (tab-separated with `IFS=$'\t' read -r ...`). Option (b) also fixes future fields cleanly.

bugs.error_message: After auto_resolve_wolf_conflicts succeeds in the wave-merge phase (run-sessions.sh:943-946), `git -C "$TRUNK_WORKTREE_DIR" commit -q -m "..." 2>/dev/null` is invoked as a bare command in the `then` branch of the if. It has no `|| true` and is not the test of an `if`, so under the script's `set -euo pipefail` (line 47), any non-zero exit (pre-commit hook failure, GPG signing failure when commit.gpgsign=true, index lock contention) terminates the runner mid-wave. The EXIT trap fires and kills jobs, but write_epic_result is never called, so the [EPIC_RESULT_START] sentinel and the result JSON file at $TMPDIR/epic-toolkit/<repo>--<name>.result.json are never written - external watchers polling that file (the documented orchestrator hand-off, including Claude/OpenCode after running the script) hang waiting for completion that never reports. EPIC_FAILED is also not set, so wave bookkeeping is inconsistent with the actual exit.
bugs.root_cause: The auto-resolve commit is the only non-guarded git mutation in the merge loop. Sibling commits in the same file use `|| true` (line 953 merge --abort) or pipe through `if` tests (line 932 merge --no-ff). The auto-resolve happy-path was written without the same guard.
bugs.fix: Wrap the commit in an `if`: succeed -> add_merged_session, fail -> set EPIC_FAILED=true, FIRST_FAILED_ID, log err, and break. Mirrors the structure of the surrounding `if git merge --no-ff ...` at line 932 and ensures the runner always reaches write_epic_result. Same set-e abruptness pattern as bug-043 / bug-046 (cleanup-skipped-on-set-e exits).

bugs.error_message: User reported epic runner skipped all session execution: '✓ session 0N already committed on this branch — skipping (use --fresh to force re-run)' for every session on a fresh epic run, total runtime 18 seconds, no code produced. The epic created worktrees, synced session prompts, made the bootstrap commit, then immediately reported every session as 'already committed' and merged only the bootstrap before exiting. --fresh worked around it.
bugs.root_cause: session_completed_on_branch ran `git log --format='%s' "$branch"` over the entire branch history. The per-session worktree (created at run-sessions.sh:860 from $BRANCH, which was just branched off $BASE_BRANCH at line 611) inherits the full base-branch history. A single commit anywhere on main whose subject begins with 'feat: Session N' or 'Merge session NN (' — common when a previously-merged epic of the same family is in main, or any unrelated commit happens to use a session-style subject — falsely matched the resume-cache regex for every session, so every run_one_session call returned early before invoking Claude. The bug fires on the very first run with no prior epic state at all; nothing in the workflow signals to the user that the cache check is poisoned by inherited history.
bugs.fix: Scope the subject scan to commits unique to the epic by using rev range `${BASE_BRANCH}..${branch}` instead of bare `$branch`. Falls back to `origin/$BASE_BRANCH..` when only the remote-tracking ref exists locally, and to the legacy unscoped scan when neither resolves so we never harden into a worse failure mode. Verified with two scenarios: (a) main carrying a poison `feat: Session 1 — old work` commit no longer triggers a false skip, and (b) a real `feat: Session 1` commit made on the epic branch is still detected as completed for legitimate resume.

bugs.error_message: UTF-8 BOM at the start of session-NN-*.md silently breaks YAML frontmatter parsing — parse_frontmatter returns {}, the session loses depends_on/parallel_safe/touches, and the entire DAG silently collapses to the implicit linear chain (one session per wave). No warning. Verified via /tmp/bom-test reproduction: two parallel_safe sessions with depends_on=[] produced 2 sequential waves instead of 1 parallel wave.
bugs.root_cause: scripts/epic-dag.py:60 — `if not text.startswith('---\n'):` fails when the file begins with the UTF-8 BOM (\ufeff). The CRLF normalization above it (lines 58-59) handles \r but not \ufeff. Files saved by Windows editors with 'UTF-8 with BOM' (Notepad's default for some encodings, VS Code's optional setting) routinely include the BOM. Same family as bug-024 (CRLF) and bug-035 (encoding).
bugs.fix: Strip a leading \ufeff before the startswith check, and mirror the same fix in extract_prompt's awk path in scripts/lib/epic-session.sh so prompt extraction also handles BOM'd files. Suggested patch in epic-dag.py: after CRLF normalization, add `if text.startswith('\ufeff'): text = text[1:]`.

bugs.error_message: Session slug containing '|' (allowed by SESSION_RE — only spaces are rejected) corrupts the pipe-delimited result row in write_epic_result and crashes the json-emit step with `ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'api'` (or similar). The entire result file fails to write, and external watchers polling the EPIC_RESULT_FILE sentinel hang indefinitely. Verified by simulating row construction with slug='auth|api'.
bugs.root_cause: scripts/lib/epic-result.sh:112 encodes the session row as `${key}|${status}|${slug}|${elapsed}|${exit_code}|${error_type}|${log_path}|${stderr_path}` and the consuming Python script splits on '|' (lines 162-172). The slug regex in scripts/epic-dag.py:48 (`^session-(\d+)-(.+)\.md$`) and the validator at line 132 only reject space — '|' (and other shell/JSON-safe chars) slip through. With slug='foo|bar', parts[2]='foo', parts[3]='bar' (instead of elapsed seconds), and `int(parts[3])` raises ValueError.
bugs.fix: Either (a) tighten the slug validator in epic-dag.py to reject '|' (and ideally restrict to [A-Za-z0-9._-]), or (b) switch the result-file row delimiter to TAB (\t) or NUL (\0) which can't appear in filenames on POSIX, or (c) shell-escape/JSON-encode the slug field before emitting. Option (a) is least invasive.

bugs.error_message: Hardcoded WAVE_TIMEOUT_MINUTES=240 silently overrides --timeout when --timeout > 240, or when a wave has many sequential sessions whose total run time exceeds 4 hours. The wave-level timeout fires first, kills sessions via kill_tree, and marks them failed/exit-137 even though their per-session --timeout hadn't elapsed. The user thinks they configured a 5-hour session timeout, but their sessions get killed at 4 hours.
bugs.root_cause: scripts/run-sessions.sh:94 sets WAVE_TIMEOUT_MINUTES=240 with no CLI flag, no .epic-config.json override, and no auto-derivation. The wave loop at line 888 (`wave_timeout=$((WAVE_TIMEOUT_MINUTES * 60))`) and line 893 (`if [[ $wave_elapsed -gt $wave_timeout ]]`) enforces the cap unconditionally. Sequential sessions within a wave (MAX_PARALLEL=1, or --sequential which only flattens to single-session waves but still each per-session bounded by wave timeout) are most affected. A 4-session sequential wave with --timeout 90 needs up to 360m, but the 240m wave ceiling kills the last sessions.
bugs.fix: Add a --wave-timeout flag and config-file key (`waveTimeout`), AND/OR auto-derive a sensible default such as `max(WAVE_TIMEOUT_MINUTES, TIMEOUT * len(IN_RANGE) / MAX_PARALLEL + buffer)` per-wave inside the loop. At minimum, log a warning when TIMEOUT > WAVE_TIMEOUT_MINUTES at startup so users aren't surprised.

bugs.error_message: git merge fails for non-conflict reasons (index.lock, dirty tree); auto_resolve_wolf_conflicts returns 2 (no conflict markers), but the else branch runs merge --abort on a non-existent merge and reports false MERGE CONFLICT, aborting the epic
bugs.root_cause: if auto_resolve_wolf_conflicts ... treats both return 1 (real conflicts) and return 2 (no markers) as failure. Return 2 means no merge-in-progress state exists, so merge --abort fails silently and the epic is aborted with a misleading error message
bugs.fix: Capture return code via _resolve_rc=0; auto_resolve_wolf_conflicts || _resolve_rc=$?. Distinguish rc=2 (no conflict markers → no merge to abort, emit a different error) from rc=1 (real conflicts → merge --abort and fail). Only call merge --abort for rc=1.

bugs.error_message: On retry after exec failure, plan phase re-runs from scratch even when a valid plan file already exists from the first attempt, wasting tokens and risking a plan-phase failure that blocks exec re-try
bugs.root_cause: plan_cached is evaluated once before the retry loop from an mtime check; it is never re-checked inside the loop. A successful plan run on attempt 1 writes plan_file, but plan_cached stays false, so attempt 2 triggers a redundant plan phase
bugs.fix: At the start of each retry iteration (attempt > 1), re-check [[ -s plan_file && plan_file -nt session_path ]] and set plan_cached=true if it passes, so subsequent attempts reuse the existing plan

bugs.error_message: PROGRESS_SCRIPT, DAG_SCRIPT, UI_SCRIPT built via $(dirname "$0") which is a relative path when run-sessions.sh is invoked with a relative path. After cd "$TRUNK_WORKTREE_DIR" and inside session subshells that cd "$wt_dir", PROGRESS_SCRIPT resolves to a non-existent path; [[ -f "$PROGRESS_SCRIPT" ]] silently returns false and run_cli falls back to no-progress mode for all sessions
bugs.root_cause: dirname "$0" is not canonicalized; lib scripts use the already-absolute $CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT but DAG_SCRIPT etc. were set independently via dirname

bugs.error_message: Wolf-json merge driver silently drops one entry on bug-id collision. Two parallel sessions in the same wave can both append a bug with the same id (each hook computes 'next free id' from its local view of buglog.json, unaware of the sibling's addition). When trunk merges the second session, merge_buglog dedupes by id and keeps only the entry with the later last_seen — the other session's real, distinct bug entry is silently lost. Reproduced with two synthetic 'bug-090' entries: only one survives the merge.
bugs.root_cause: merge_buglog (lines 36-51) treats id collisions as duplicates rather than as concurrent allocations. The dedup loop unconditionally overwrites by_id[bid] when last_seen comparison favors the newer entry; when the two entries are not duplicates of the same event but are independent additions that happened to pick the same id, the older one is dropped without warning. The merge driver is the only safety net for the parallel-wave scenario the runner explicitly enables.
bugs.fix: Detect non-duplicate id collisions and re-id one side instead of dropping it. When both ours[bid] and theirs[bid] exist and differ on substantive fields (timestamp, error_message, file, root_cause), keep both: assign the newer one the next free id past max(all-ids). Alternatively, switch the bug id allocator to a content-addressable form (hash of timestamp+file+error) so two concurrent additions naturally hash to different ids.

bugs.error_message: BRANCH_SANITIZED produces single-dash output instead of the documented double-dash. The header docstring (lines 14-18) advertises layout '.epic-worktrees/<repo>/epic--<name>/' for the trunk worktree, but a branch name 'epic/foo' actually produces directory 'epic-foo' (single dash). The downstream '--s' separator that distinguishes trunk from per-session worktree dirs still works, but the trunk dir name disagrees with documentation and any external tooling that scans the worktree base for 'epic--*' will miss the trunk.
bugs.root_cause: Line 339: BRANCH_SANITIZED="$(echo "$BRANCH" | tr '/' '--')". tr maps SET1 chars to SET2 chars positionally; with SET1='/' (one char) and SET2='--' (two chars), only SET2[0]='-' is used. SET2[1] is silently discarded. The author appears to have expected tr to substitute each '/' with the literal two-char string '--', which is sed's behavior, not tr's. Verified: 'epic/foo/bar' produces 'epic-foo-bar' instead of the intended 'epic--foo--bar'.
bugs.fix: Replace with sed: BRANCH_SANITIZED="$(echo "$BRANCH" | sed 's|/|--|g')". Mostly cosmetic since the only place BRANCH_SANITIZED is used as a glob anchor (lines 583, 1326) and a directory name (lines 610, 838) is consistent with itself, but the divergence from the docstring is real and any future tool that searches '$WORKTREE_BASE/epic--*' for trunk worktrees will find nothing.

bugs.error_message: set -- $_rest in the SESSION-line parser glob-expands tokens before they are consumed, silently corrupting session metadata when a slug contains '*', '?', '[', or ']' AND the cwd happens to have files matching that pattern. Reproduced: with cwd containing foo-thing.txt and foo-other.txt, parsing a SESSION line whose slug field is 'foo*' produced $5='foo-other.txt', $6='foo-thing.txt', shifting model and cli into the wrong slots. The slug becomes a filename, parallel-safe becomes another filename, model becomes '1', cli becomes '-' — none of the downstream consumers detect the corruption.
bugs.root_cause: Line 423: set -- $_rest deliberately leaves _rest unquoted to enable word-splitting on whitespace, but bash's default behavior also performs pathname (glob) expansion on each unquoted word. The slug validator in scripts/epic-dag.py (lines 132-142) only rejects space, pipe, and tab — characters that break field-delimited parsers — and explicitly lists 'space breaks set -- $_rest' as the rationale, but missed that the SAME parser is also vulnerable to glob expansion. Same class as bug-082 (pipe-in-slug) but for a different breaking character.
bugs.fix: Either disable globbing locally before the parser ('set -f' before set --, 'set +f' after) so that '*', '?', '[' are treated as literals; or tighten the slug validator in epic-dag.py to also reject '*', '?', '[', ']', and any other shell-meta or filesystem-fragile characters (effectively whitelist [A-Za-z0-9._-] for slugs). Validator-tightening is the safer fix because it also protects sess_branch and sess_wt_dir_name construction at lines 837-838.

bugs.error_message: Startup stale-worktree cleanup at scripts/run-sessions.sh:587-606 silently destroys per-session worktrees that the per-wave cleanup explicitly refuses to touch. The startup pass globs '${BRANCH_SANITIZED}--s[0-9]*-*' and calls 'git worktree remove --force || rm -rf' on any whose session id is no longer in the current DAG, with NO is_worktree_in_use() check and NO ahead-of-trunk commit check. The per-wave cleanup at 850-868 has BOTH guards (mirrors bugs 017/060/064 hardening) — same operation, same risk, but only one path enforces them. Repro: epic with session-09 fails mid-run leaving committed-but-unmerged work on epic/myepic--s09-foo and untracked plan/log artefacts in the worktree; user removes session-09-*.md from the sessions dir and re-runs the epic; the next startup deletes the worktree (work in untracked files is gone, branch ref survives but the dirstate is destroyed) without any prompt, error, or log line beyond a routine '↻ removing stale worktree'. Also unsafe under concurrent runners sharing WORKTREE_BASE — a sibling runner's in-flight worktree gets ripped out from under it.
bugs.root_cause: Two cleanup code-paths grew independently. Per-wave cleanup (added during the bug-017/060/064 hardening waves) gained is_worktree_in_use() and the rev-list ahead check; the startup-stale pass — which existed before that hardening and is rarely thought about because it only fires when sessions disappear from the DAG — never received the same treatment. Comment at lines 568-573 only justifies SCOPING the glob to the current epic (avoiding cross-epic deletion), not safety inside the current epic.
bugs.fix: Refactor the deletion into a shared helper (e.g. safe_remove_session_worktree wt_dir branch_name) that performs is_worktree_in_use() and the BRANCH..sess_branch ahead check, and have BOTH the startup pass and the per-wave pass call it. For the startup pass, treat the per-wave 'exit 1 with explanation' on guard-failure as a 'warn + skip' instead — orphaned worktrees should be reported, not silently destroyed.

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py parse_frontmatter accepts only literal 'true'/'false' as YAML booleans; YAML 1.1 boolean-strings ('yes', 'no', 'on', 'off', 'y', 'n', plus capitalisations) fall through to the string branch, then load_sessions does bool(fm.get('parallel_safe', True)). bool('no') / bool('No') / bool('off') / bool('n') all return True (non-empty string), so a user who writes 'parallel_safe: no' to force solo-execution actually gets parallel_safe=True and the session runs concurrently with siblings — exactly the opposite of the declared intent. Reproduced directly against the parser: parallel_safe values 'no', 'off', 'n', 'No' all stored as raw strings then coerced to True by bool(). Symptom in the field is silent: the user sees the session scheduled into a parallel wave, no error, and (if touches overlap) downstream merge conflicts that look like a DAG-design problem rather than a frontmatter-parsing problem.
bugs.root_cause: Lines 113-114: 'elif v.lower() in ("true", "false"): result[k] = v.lower() == "true"'. The whitelist is too narrow — YAML 1.1 (and the long tail of editors / linters / docs that still emit YAML 1.1 syntax) treats yes/no/on/off as booleans. The session_dag.py boolean coercion via bool() at line 191 then masks the type mismatch: a non-empty string is truthy regardless of intended meaning, so 'no' → True. There is no defensive check that catches 'parallel_safe was a string when it should have been bool'.
bugs.fix: Two-part: (1) extend the boolean detection in parse_frontmatter to accept the YAML 1.1 set {true,false,yes,no,on,off,y,n} case-insensitively and map yes/y/on→True, no/n/off→False; (2) in load_sessions, when fm.get('parallel_safe') is a STRING that isn't one of the recognised boolean spellings, raise SystemExit with a clear message rather than silently coercing via bool(). Same defence applies to any future bool-typed frontmatter field.

bugs.error_message: wolf-json merge driver _bug_signature includes 'timestamp' as the first key, breaking same-bug deduplication when two parallel sessions detect the SAME root-cause within sub-second of each other. Each session computes the next free bug id from its local view (already covered by bug-090's id-collision-renumber fix), assigns the same id, but produces different ISO timestamps. merge_buglog sees collision-on-id, _bug_signature comparison fails on the timestamp field even though error_message + file + root_cause are identical, falls into the 'different events that merely collided on id' branch, and renumbers the second entry to a new free id. Result: two distinct bug entries (bug-100 and bug-101) describe one event, occurrences:1 each instead of being merged into a single entry with occurrences:2. Reproduced directly: two synthetic ours/theirs blobs with identical error/file/cause but timestamps 333ms apart produced both bug-100 AND bug-101 in the merged output.
bugs.root_cause: Line 36-42: _bug_signature returns (timestamp, error_message, file, root_cause). Including timestamp makes it impossible for two near-simultaneous detections of the same bug to dedupe — yet that is exactly the parallel-session case the merge driver exists for. The bug-090 fix correctly stopped silent data loss on id collision, but the keep-or-renumber decision keys on a signature that two truly-duplicate concurrent detections will never share.
bugs.fix: Remove 'timestamp' from _bug_signature so the dedup key is (error_message, file, root_cause) — the semantic identity of the bug. Keep the existing 'newer last_seen wins' replacement logic, and ALSO accumulate occurrences (sum both sides' occurrences fields) when the signatures match — otherwise a true-duplicate collapse loses the occurrence count from the side that didn't win. Without this fix, every concurrent log of the same bug class compounds into more entries on every parallel wave.
